i   
**NATIONAL GOALS OF EDUCATION** 

Education in Kenya should: 

**1\. Foster nationalism and patriotism and promote national unity**   
The people of Kenya belong to different communities, races and religions, but these differences need not divide them. They must be able  to live and interact as Kenyans. It is a paramount duty of education to help young people acquire this sense of nationhood by resolving conflicts and promoting positive attitudes of mutual respect, to live together in harmony and foster patriotism, and make a positive  contribution to the life of the nation. 

**2\. Promote the social, economic, technological, and industrial needs for national development**   
Education should prepare the youth of the country to play an effective and productive role in the life of the nation. **a) Social Needs**   
Education in Kenya must prepare children for changes in attitudes and relationships that are necessary for the smooth progress of a  rapidly developing modern economy. In the wake of rapid modernisation, there is bound to be a silent social revolution. Education  should assist our youth to adapt to this change. 

**b) Economic Needs**    
Education in Kenya should produce citizens with the skills, knowledge, expertise, and personal qualities required to support a growing  economy. Kenya is building a modern and independent economy that needs an adequate and relevant domestic workforce. **c) Technological and Industrial Needs**    
Education in Kenya should provide learners with the necessary skills and attitudes for industrial development. Kenya recognises the  rapid industrial and technological changes taking place, especially in the developed world. We can only be part of this development  if our education system is deliberately focused on the knowledge, skills and attitudes that will prepare our young people for these  changing global trends. 

**3\. Promote individual development and self-fulfillment**   
Education should provide opportunities for the fullest development of individual talents and personality. It should help children to develop  their potential interests and abilities. A vital aspect of individual development is the building of character. 

**4\. Promote sound moral and religious values.**   
Education should provide for the development of knowledge, skills and attitudes that will enhance the acquisition of sound moral values  and help children to grow up into self-disciplined, self-reliant and integrated citizens.

ii   
**5\. Promote social equality and responsibility.**    
Education should promote social equality and foster a sense of social responsibility within an education system that provides equal  educational opportunities for all. It should give all children varied and challenging opportunities for collective activities and corporate  social service irrespective of gender, ability or geographical environment.  

**6\. Promote respect for and development of Kenya’s rich and varied cultures.**   
Education should instill in the youth of Kenya an understanding of past and present cultures and their valid place in contemporary society.  Children should be able to blend the best of traditional values with the changing requirements that must follow rapid development to build  a stable and modern society.  

**7\. Promote international consciousness and foster positive attitudes towards other nations.**    
Kenya is part of the international community. It is part of the complicated and interdependent network of peoples and nations. Education  should therefore lead the youth of the country to accept membership of this international community with all the obligations and  responsibilities, rights and benefits that this membership entails. 

**8\. Promote positive attitudes towards good health and environmental protection.**   
Education should inculcate in young people the value of good health for them to avoid indulging in activities that will lead to physical or  mental ill health. It should foster positive attitudes towards environmental development and conservation. It should lead the youth of  Kenya to appreciate the need for a healthy environment.

iii   
**THE SENIOR SCHOOL IN THE COMPETENCY BASED CURRICULUM**  

Senior School is the fourth level of Basic Education in the Competency Based Curriculum (CBC) that the learner shall proceed to after the  Pre-Primary School, Primary School and Junior School levels. The essence of Senior School is to offer learners a pre-tertiary/university/ pre career experience in which the learners have an opportunity to choose pathways, tracks and subjects where they shall have demonstrated  interest and/or potential at the earlier levels. Senior School comprises three years of education for learners generally in the 16-18 years age  bracket and lays the foundation for further education and training at the tertiary level and the world of work. In the CBC vision, learners exiting  this level are expected to be ***engaged, empowered and ethical citizens*** ready to participate in the socio-economic development of the nation. 

At this level, the learner shall take seven (7) learning areas as recommended by the *Presidential Working Party on Educational Reforms* (PWPER, 2023). These shall comprise **four compulsory learning areas** namely: English, Kiswahili, Essential Mathematics/Core  Mathematics and Community Service Learning (CSL). Learners will choose between Core Mathematics (for learners pursuing the Science  Technology Engineering and Mathematics Pathway) and Essential Mathematics (for learners pursuing the non-STEM pathways). The learner  will select three learning areas depending on their career choice, aptitude, interest and personality with guidance by the Senior School career  teacher.  

**LEARNING OUTCOMES FOR SENIOR SCHOOL**  

By the end of Senior School, the learner should be able to:    
1\. communicate effectively and utilise information and communication technology across varied contexts,  2\. apply mathematical, logical and critical thinking skills for problem solving, 

3\. apply basic research and scientific skills to manipulate the environment and solve problems,    
4\. exploit individual talents for leisure, self-fulfilment, career growth, further education and training,  5\. uphold national, moral and religious values and apply them in day-to-day life,  

6\. apply and promote health care strategies in day-to-day life,    
7\. protect, preserve and improve the environment for sustainability,    
8\. demonstrate active local and global citizenship for harmonious co-existence,    
9\. demonstrate appreciation of diversity in people and cultures,    
10\. manage pertinent and contemporary issues responsibly.

iv   
**TABLE 1: LIST OF SUBJECTS AT SENIOR SCHOOL** 

| Core Subjects  | Arts & Sports   Science | Social Sciences  | Science, Technology, Engineering &  Mathematics (STEM) |
| ----- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| 1\. English  2\. Kiswahili/KSL  3\. Core Mathematics/   Essential Mathematics 4\. Community Service  Learning (CSL) | 5\. Sports and   Recreation  6\. Music and Dance 7\. Theatre and Film 8\. Fine Arts | 9\. Literature in English  10\. Indigenous Languages  11\. Fasihi ya Kiswahili  12\. Sign Language  13\. Arabic  14\. French  15\. German  16\. Mandarin Chinese   17\. Christian Religious Education 18\. Islamic Religious Education 19\. Hindu Religious Education 20\. Business Studies  21\. History and Citizenship  22\. Geography | 23\. Biology  24\. Chemistry  25\. Physics  26\. General Science  27\. Agriculture  28\. Computer Studies  29\. Home Science   30\. Aviation   31\. Building Construction  32\. Electricity  33\. Metalwork  34\. Power Mechanics  35\. Woodwork  36\. Media Technology  37\. Marine and Fisheries Technology |

**Note:**   
**a)** Physical Education (PE) and Information Communication and Technology (ICT) will be offered to all learners to facilitate learning,  enjoyment and acquisition of life skills.  

**b)** Every school shall offer Pastoral/Religious Programme of Instruction (P/RPI) to enhance moral, spiritual and character development  of the learners.  

**c)** There shall be one (1) lesson for personal or group study to deepen knowledge and enhance skills in areas of choice by the learners. **Table 2 provides guidelines on distribution of lessons at Senior School.**

v

| 2\. Kiswahili  | 5 |
| 3\. Essential Mathematics/Core Mathematics  | 5 |
| 4\. Community Service Learning  | 3 |
| **Elective Subjects** |  |
| 5\. Elective 1  | 5 |
| 6\. Elective 2  | 5 |
| 7\. Elective 3  | 5 |
| \* *Physical Education*  | *3* |
| *\* ICT Skills*  | *2* |
| *\*PPI*  | *1* |
| *\* Learner Personal /Group Study*  | *1* |
| **Total Number of Lessons**  | **40** |

vi   
**ESSENCE STATEMENT** 

Arabic is one of the ancient and living languages. It is among the six major languages of the world. Arabic, one of the official  languages of the United Nations (UN) and the African Union (AU), is spoken by an estimated population of over 500 million  people worldwide. It is also the language in which the Holy Quran and other Islamic literatures are written, making it essential for  Muslims to learn. The Arabic language is important for contemporary developments such as mass movements of people across  the globe, an increase in diverse, multicultural societies, rapid technological changes, and increased economic globalisation.  Arabic-speaking countries account for approximately 5-8 % of the global GDP.  

Learning the Arabic language will open wide opportunities for learners in national and international arenas. A foreign language is  acquired through understandable and meaningful exposure, as observed by Stephen Kreshen’s foreign language actualisation  theory. Through learning the Arabic language, learners will develop an intercultural capability and an understanding of the role of  language and culture in communication and become more receptive to differences and diversity. They will develop an  understanding of global citizenship and reflect on their heritage, values, culture, and identity. 

Arabic language in Senior School will build on the skills developed in Junior School and form the basis for further language  development and specialisation at the Tertiary Level and the World of Work. The learner will be expected to exit this level with  an intermediate proficiency and competence in the language. 

vii   
**SUBJECT GENERAL LEARNING OUTCOMES**  

By the end of Senior School, the learner should be able to:   
1\. use Arabic as a communication tool through the four language skills of listening, speaking, reading and writing to meet  basic and practical communication needs. 

2\. develop an ability to speak fluently and accurately in a variety of situations.   
3\. develop a reading culture for information and pleasure; drawing inferences and relating texts to previous knowledge;  reading critically and developing the confidence to ask and answer questions. 

4\. develop and apply skills and strategies for idea generation, selection, development and organization in writing. 5\. apply the grammatical rules, structures, and linguistic functions for real life purposes. 

6\. understand and apply the overarching values and pertinent and contemporary issues embedded in the curriculum. 7\. realise the uniqueness of the Arabic culture, heritage and its contribution to the world of knowledge.

viii   
**SUMMARY OF STRANDS AND SUBSTRANDS**

| Strands  | Sub Strands |
| :---- | :---- |
| **Listening and Speaking**  | Active Listening  Phonological awareness  Speaking Fluency  Imitative Speaking  Conversational skills  Selective Listening |
| **Reading**  | Reading for Comprehension  Reading Fluency  Reading for Comprehension  Intensive reading: Short Arabic Stories  Reading for Comprehension  Independent Reading |
| **Writing**  | Guided Writing: Sequence and Coherence  The Writing Process  Mechanics of Writing: Spelling and Punctuation  Social Writing: Email, Recipes, Advertisement, Poster  Creative Writing: Imaginative composition  Functional Writing: Formal Letters |
| **Grammar**  | *Nawasikh (kaana wa akhwatuha* and *inna wa akhwatuha)*  Subjunctives (*An-nawaasib*) and Jussives (*Al-jawaazim*)  Negative Articles *(Adawatu nnafyi)*  Five Special Nouns *(Asmaa ul Khamsa)*  Five special verbs *(Af ’ aal al Khamsa)*  Numbers and Quantity *(Al- ’adad wal Ma’duud)* |

ix 

| THEME 1: SOCIAL LIFE (االجتماعية الحياة – )FRIENDS )أصدقاء) |  |  |  |  |
| ----- | ----- | ----- | ----- | ----- |
| **Strand**  | **Sub Strand**  | **Specific Learning   Outcomes** | **Suggested Learning Experiences**  | **Suggested Key  Inquiry   Question(s)** |
| **1.0 Listening  and Speaking** | **1.1 Active   Listening**   ∙ *Main idea*  ∙ *Active   listening skills*  ∙ *Simple   questions* | By the end of the Sub Strand,  the learner should be able to: a) identify the main idea in  an oral text,   b) explore non-verbal cues that depict active   listening for effective   communication,  c) respond to questions  from an oral text for   information,  d) value active listening for  effective communication. | The learner is guided to:  ∙ listen to an audio presentation on  introduction of friends,  ∙ extract information on how to introduce friends from the audio, ∙ role play a scene to introduce  friends to each other,  ∙ discuss actions that show active  listening (*e.g. nodding, taking   notes, maintaining eye contact,  asking questions, avoiding   distractions*),  ∙ team up to ask and answer   questions about each other’s likes  and dislikes,  ∙ work jointly to answer direct  questions from a given oral text,  ∙ role play an active listening  session and peer assess each   other’s performance for   correctness.  | 1\. Why is it   important   to actively   listen to   others?  2\. How is   active   listening   different   from   passive   listening? |

1

| Core competencies to be developed:  ∙ Digital literacy: This is promoted as the learner interacts with digital content when listening to an audio presentation on  introduction of friends.  ∙ Learning to learn: This is promoted as the learner builds on their learning experiences when peer assessing each other’s  role play on active listening for correctness. |
| :---- |
| **Values:**   ∙ Integrity: This is enhanced as the learner displays honesty in feedback offered when peer assessing each other’s work for  correctness.  ∙ Unity: This is promoted as the learner displays a team spirit as they collaborate with each other to ask and answer questions about their likes and dislikes. |
| **Pertinent and Contemporary Issues (PCIs):**   Healthy interpersonal relationships: This is addressed as the learners build their social awareness skills when interacting  actively with friends and the social life. |

2

| Strand  | Sub Strand  | Specific Learning Outcomes  | Suggested Learning Experiences  | Suggested Key  Inquiry   Question(s) |
| ----- | ----- | :---- | ----- | :---- |
| **2.0 Reading**  | **2.1 Reading for  Comprehension**  ∙ *Skimming for  main idea*  ∙ *Scanning for  specific ideas*   ∙ *Articulation* | By the end of the Sub Strand,  the learner should be able to: a) identify the main idea   in a text for   information,  b) explain the specific   ideas from a text for   comprehension,  c) read texts aloud for   accurate articulation,   d) develop interest in   reading Arabic texts   for enjoyment.  | The learner is guided to:  ∙ read varied sentences aloud  about friends from a given   digital display,  ∙ take turns to read short   dialogues on introduction of   others (friends) aloud,   ∙ work jointly to discuss the main  idea from the dialogue read,  ∙ deduce specific ideas from the  dialogue (*e.g. names of   speakers, what they like, where  they live*) and share with peers,  ∙ conduct a reader’s theatre to  read short paragraphs on   hobbies and interests of friends  aloud,  ∙ peer assess each other’s reading  for correct articulation of words. | How does   reading aloud   enrich reading   skills? |
| **Core competencies to be developed:**  ∙ Critical thinking and problem solving: This is enhanced as the learner applies logical reasoning when deducing specific  ideas from the dialogue. |  |  |  |  |

3

| ∙ Digital literacy: This is promoted as the learner interacts with technology when reading varied sentences aloud on the  theme of friends from a given digital display. |
| :---- |
| **Values:**   ∙ Patriotism: This is promoted as the learner exhibits honesty when peer assessing each other’s reading for correct  articulation of words.  ∙ Respect: This is nurtured as the learner exhibits patience with others when conducting a reader’s theatre to read short  paragraphs on hobbies and interests of friends aloud. |
| **Pertinent and Contemporary Issues (PCIs):**  Peace Education: This is promoted as the learners interact with texts and information on social life as a way of enhancing  peaceful coexistence. |

4

| Strand  | Sub Strand  | Specific Learning Outcomes  | Suggested Learning Experiences  | Suggested Key   Inquiry   Question(s) |
| :---- | ----- | ----- | ----- | :---- |
| **3.0   Writing**  | **3.1 Guided   Writing**   ∙ *Neatness   and   legibility*  ∙ *Coherence* | By the end of the Sub Strand, the learner should be able to: a) construct sentences in   neat and legible   handwriting,  b) compose a coherent   paragraph for effective   communication,  c) develop interest in   writing Arabic legibly   and neatly. | The learner is guided to:  ∙ collaborate to list vocabulary  learnt related to the theme on   friends,   ∙ write short sentences about friends and their likes and dislikes in neat  handwriting,   ∙ use a substitution table to form coherent sentences,  ∙ reorder jumbled up words to form  a coherent sentence and share with  peers,  ∙ reorganise jumbled up sentences to  form a coherent paragraph,   ∙ team up to write a paragraph  neatly and display in class (*check  on character/letter shapes and  sizes, word spacing and general  layout*),   ∙ peer assess each other’s   paragraphs for neatness,  ∙ organise their coherent and neatly  written paragraphs in a portfolio. | Why should we   write texts   neatly and   legibly? |

5

| Core competencies to be developed:  ∙ Creativity and imagination: This is enhanced as the learner embraces creative ideas when writing neat paragraphs and  displaying in class.  ∙ Self efficacy: This is promoted as the learner shows concerted attention to detail when reordering jumbled up words to  form a coherent sentence and shares with peers. |
| :---- |
| **Values:**   ∙ Responsibility: This is enhanced as the learner is made aware of their own responsibilities in tasks when collaborating to  list vocabulary learnt related to friends.  ∙ Peace: This is demonstrated as the learner resolves differences in opinions amicably when peer assessing each other’s  paragraphs for correctness. |
| **Pertinent and Contemporary Issues (PCIs):**  Group dynamics: This is addressed as the learners work jointly with peers of different personalities and abilities to accomplish  tasks and achieve group goals. |

| **4.0 Grammar**  | **4.1 Nawasikh**   ∙ *Kana wa   akhawatuha*  )كان وأخواتها(  ∙ *Inna wa   akhawatuha*  )إ ّن وأخواتها( | By the end of the Sub Strand,  the learner should be able to:  a) discuss the functions of   *kaana wa akhwatuha* and *inna wa akhwatuha* for   information,  b) construct nominal   sentences using *kaana wa  akhwatuha* and *inna wa  akhwatuha,*  c) value the role of *nawasikh* in Arabic language. | The learner is guided to:  ∙ list *kaana wa akhwatuha* and *inna  wa akhawatuha* from a given text, ∙ work jointly to discuss the   functions of *kaana wa akhwatuha* and *inna wa akhwatuha* in a text, ∙ construct simple sentences using  *kaana wa akhwatuha* and *inna wa  akhawatuha* and share with peers  for review,  ∙ fill in blank spaces in sentences  using *kaana wa akhwatuha* and *inna wa akhawatuha,*  ∙ use *kaana wa akhwatuha* and *inna  wa akhawatuha* in singular, dual  and plural sentences and make the  necessary changes. | How does *kaana wa akhwatuha*  and *inna wa   akhwatuha* affect  sentence   structure? |

| Strand  | Sub Strand  | Specific Learning   Outcomes | Suggested Learning Experiences  | Suggested Key  Inquiry   Question(s) |
| :---- | ----- | ----- | ----- | :---- |
| **4.0 Grammar**  | **4.2 Subjunctive  (*An-nawaasib*) and  Jussive (*Al  jawaazim*) moods**  ∙ *Subjunctives (An nawaasib)*  الَّن  ِصب  َوا  ∙ *Jussives (Al  jawaazim)*  َو ِز ال  ا م َج  | By the end of the sub  strand, the learner should  be able to:   a) distinguish  subjunctives *(An  nawaasib)* and   jussives *(Al  jawaazim)* for   information,  b) use *An-nawaasib* and  *Al-jawaazim* in   sentences for effective  communication,   c) acknowledge the role  of *An-nawaasib* and   *Al-jawaazim* in   language acquisition. | The learner is guided to:  ∙ brainstorm on the concept of  subjunctives *(An-nawaasib)* and  jussives *(Al-jawaazim)* with peers*,*   ∙ use a graphic organiser (*e.g. mind  maps, pictures and diagrams*) to  differentiate between *An  nawaasib* and *Al-jawaazim* and  present to peers*,*  ∙ discuss how subjunctives and  jussives are formed,  ∙ work collaboratively to list examples of *An-nawaasib* and *Al jawaazim* on flashcards*,*  ∙ construct sentences using *An nawaasib* and *Al-jawaazim,*  ∙ peer assess each other’s sentences  for correctness. | What is the   effect of *An  nawaasib* and  *Al-jawaazim* in  a verbal   sentence? |
| **Core competencies to be developed:**  ∙ Creativity and imagination: This is enhanced as the learner embraces ideas that inspire creative thinking when using a  graphic organiser to differentiate between *An-nawaasib* and *Al-jawaazim* and presents to peers.  ∙ Self-efficacy: This is enhanced as the learner is made aware of their ability and areas of growth through peer assessment of  sentences for correctness. |  |  |  |  |

15

| Values:   ∙ Patriotism: This is promoted as the learner advocates for harmonious relationships among peers when brainstorming on the  concept of *An-nawaasib* and *Al-jawaazim*.  ∙ Responsibility: This is nurtured as the learner engages in assigned tasks in teams when working collaboratively to list *An nawaasib* and *Al-jawaazim* on flashcards. |
| :---- |
| **Pertinent and Contemporary Issues (PCIs):**   Nationalism: This is promoted as the learners appreciate each other’s contribution and effort towards achievement of the  group goals. |

16

| )َو َسائِل ال ُم َوا َصََلت( TRANSPORT OF MEANS( – ال ِ سيَا َحة( TOURISM 3: THEME |  |  |  |  |
| :---- | ----- | ----- | ----- | :---- |
| **Strand**  | **Sub Strand**  | **Specific Learning Outcomes**  | **Suggested Learning Experiences**  | **Suggested Key   Inquiry   Question(s)** |
| **1.0 Listening  and   Speaking**  | **1.3 Speaking   Fluency**  ∙ *Accuracy*  ∙ *Stress and   intonation*  ∙ *Facial   expression   and gestures* | By the end of the Sub Strand,  the learner should be able to: a) pronounce simple   sentences in oral texts   for accuracy,   b) use appropriate stress and  intonation in sentences for  fluency,  c) apply facial expressions and gestures when   speaking for effective   communication,  d) develop interest in   speaking Arabic fluently.  | The learner is guided to:  ∙ observe from online or offline  sources the different means of  transport,  ∙ discuss the common means of  transport in their locality and  road safety measures,  ∙ pick out simple sentences from  an audio recording and present  to peers,  ∙ orally construct simple   sentences using appropriate  stress and intonation,  ∙ use accurate facial expressions and gestures to show emotion  when speaking,  ∙ peer assess each other’s work  for correctness,  ∙ make a presentation in class on  risky road behaviors (*such as*  | How does stress   and intonation help  in speech fluency? |

17

|  |  |  | *playing on or near the road*)  while paying attention to   intonation and stress,   ∙ debate on different means of  road transport and regulations  governing road safety. |  |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | ----- | :---- |
| **Core competencies to be developed:**  ∙ Digital Literacy: This is promoted as the learner interacts with digital content when observing different means of transport  from online or offline sources.  ∙ Self-efficacy: This is enhanced as the learner clearly exhibits mastery of content and language as they make a presentation  in class on risky road behaviors (*such as playing on or near the road*). |  |  |  |  |
| **Values:**  ∙ Responsibility: This is promoted as the learner seeks accountability when presenting their work to peers for assessment. ∙ Love: This is nurtured as the learner shares their ideas jointly when discussing the common means of transport in their  locality. |  |  |  |  |
| **Pertinent and Contemporary Issues (PCIs):**   Social-economic (road safety): This is addressed as the learner interacts with texts and digital information on means of  transport and road safety measures. |  |  |  |  |

18

| Strand  | Sub Strand  | Specific Learning Outcomes  | Suggested Learning Experiences  | Suggested Key  Inquiry   Question(s) |
| :---- | ----- | ----- | ----- | :---- |
| **2.0   Reading**  | **2.3 Reading for  Comprehension**   ∙ *Prediction*  ∙ *Inferring   meaning of   vocabulary*  ∙ *Visualising* | By the end of the Sub Strand,  the learner should be able to:  a) predict events in a story  for comprehension,  b) infer meaning of   vocabulary in a given   text,  c) summarise information  using visuals for   accuracy,  d) develop interest in   reading Arabic texts for  enjoyment. | The learner is guided to:  ∙ use the title and illustrations in a  short story to predict the events  before reading,   ∙ identify vocabulary related to the  theme from a text,   ∙ infer meaning of the vocabulary  using contextual clues (nearby   words),  ∙ team up to fill in a word puzzle  using vocabulary learnt on the   theme,  ∙ interpret visuals (*diagrams,  pictures, charts, images*) to derive  meaning,  ∙ summarise information from a text  into a visual (*diagrams, pictures,  charts, images, etc.*)  ∙ display their visuals in class for a  gallery walk.  | 1.How do visuals enhance   understanding of  a text?  2\. How can you  derive meaning of  a new word in a  text without using  a dictionary? |
| **Core competencies to be developed:**  ∙ Learning to learn: This is nurtured as the learner is motivated to learn continuously as they interpret visuals (*diagrams,  pictures, charts, images*) to derive meaning.  |  |  |  |  |

19

| ∙ Critical thinking and problem solving: This is enhanced as the learner explores different options when interpreting visuals  (*diagrams, pictures, charts, images*) to derive meaning. |
| :---- |
| **Values**:  ∙ Integrity: This is enhanced as the learner displays transparency when displaying their visuals in class for a gallery walk for  all to see.  ∙ Social Justice: This is promoted as the learner accords each other an equal opportunity when teaming up to fill in a word  puzzle using vocabulary learnt on the theme. |
| **Pertinent and Contemporary Issues (PCIs):**  Social Cohesion: This is addressed as the learners work collaboratively with others to accomplish group goals. |

20

| Strand  | Sub Strand  | Specific Learning Outcomes  | Suggested Learning Experiences  | Suggested Key  Inquiry   Question(s) |
| :---- | ----- | ----- | ----- | :---- |
| **3.0 Writing**  | **3.3 The Writing  Process**  ∙ *The steps:*  \- *Pre-writing/   planning*  \- *Drafting*  \- *Revising*  \- *Editing and  proofreading*  \- *Publishing* | By the end of the Sub Strand,  the learner should be able to: a) describe the five steps of  the writing process for   information,   b) apply the five steps of the  writing process to create a  text for effective   communication,   c) acknowledge the writing  process in enhancing   Arabic writing skills. | The learner is guided to:  ∙ team up to conduct a research  from online and offline sources  on the five writing steps (*pre  writing, drafting, revising, editing  and proofreading, publishing*), ∙ work jointly to prepare a mind  map or visual organiser showing  the writing process and display in  class,   ∙ brainstorm on various means of  transport and share with peers, ∙ prepare a **plan** to write a   paragraph related to the theme by  outlining points in note form,  ∙ **draft** the first version of the  paragraph,  ∙ **revise** the draft paragraph by  making changes in case of any  errors noted,  ∙ share their draft with peers for  **editing** and **proofreading**, | How does the  writing process  enhance  writing skills? |

21

|  |  |  | ∙ write a fine and final copy of the  paragraph in neat and legible   handwriting,   ∙ work jointly to display their well  written paragraphs in class for  peer review. |  |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | ----- | :---- |
| **Core competencies to be developed:**  ∙ Creativity and imagination: This is nurtured as the learner makes connections between concepts when preparing a mind  map or visual organiser showing the writing process for display in class.  ∙ Digital Literacy: This is promoted as the learner interacts with digital content when conducting a research from online and  offline sources on the five writing steps. |  |  |  |  |
| **Values**:  ∙ Respect: This is nurtured as the learner appreciates diverse opinions from others when brainstorming on various means of  transport and shares with peers.  ∙ Unity: This is enhanced as the learner displays a team spirit while working jointly to display their well written paragraphs  in class for peer review. |  |  |  |  |
| **Pertinent and Contemporary Issues (PCIs):**   ICT: This is promoted as the learners interacts with technology and digital content while carrying out research on the writing  process. |  |  |  |  |

22

| Strand  | Sub Strand  | Specific Learning Outcomes  | Suggested Learning Experiences  | Suggested Key  Inquiry   Question(s) |
| ----- | ----- | ----- | ----- | :---- |
| **4.0 Grammar**  | **4.3 Negative  Articles   *(Adawatu   nnafyi)*  أدوات ال نفي** | By the end of the sub strand,  the learner should be able to:  a) identify negative articles   *(Adawatu nnafyi)* in texts  for information,   b) use *Adawatu nnafyi* to  construct sentences,  c) appreciate the role of  *Adawatu nnafyi* in day to  day life for effective   communication. | The learner is guided to:  ∙ brainstorm on the meaning of  *Adawatu nnafyi* in Arabic   grammar,  ∙ work jointly to pick out *Adawatu  nnafyi* from sentences,   ∙ fill in gaps to form correct   sentences using *Adawatu nnafyi,* ∙ construct sentences using *Adawatu  nnafyi* on means of transport,  ∙ team up to read a paragraph on  means of transport and highlight  *Adawatu nnafyi* from sentences,  ∙ role play a scene on road safety  while using *Adawatu nnafyi* in oral  sentences,  ∙ peer assess each other’s sentences  for correctness. | How does   *Adawatu nnafyi*  affect meaning in  texts? |
| **Core competencies to be developed:**  ∙ Citizenship: This is embraced as the learner accommodates and shows tolerance with others when role playing a scene on  road safety using *Adawatu nnafyi* in oral sentences.  ∙ Learning to learn: This is developed as the learner reflects on their own learning and makes adjustments accordingly when  peer assessing each other’s sentences for correctness. |  |  |  |  |

23

| Values:  ∙ Respect: This is demonstrated as the learner accords other’s fair chances when working jointly to pick out *Adawatu nnafyi* from sentences.  ∙ Love: This is nurtured as the learner displays a caring attitude to others by giving constructive feedback when peer  assessing sentences for correctness. |
| :---- |
| **Pertinent and Contemporary Issues (PCIs):**   Environmental awareness (infrastructural facilities): This is promoted as the learner is made aware of transport facilities and  uses vocabulary learnt related to this theme to make sentences. |

24

| )ال ِ ص َح HEALTH 4: THEMEِ َياقَة البَ َدِنَّية َوال WELLNESS AND FITNESS – ة( )الل عَافِيَة(  |  |  |  |  |
| :---- | ----- | ----- | ----- | :---- |
| **Strand**  | **Sub Strand**  | **Specific Learning Outcomes**  | **Suggested Learning Experiences**  | **Suggested Key  Inquiry   Question(s)** |
| **1.0 Listening  and   Speaking**  | **1.4 Imitative   speaking**  ∙ *Vocabulary  building*   ∙ *Oral   presentation* | By the end of the Sub Strand,  the learner should be able to: a) identify vocabulary related  to the theme for   information,  b) use variety of words to  make sentences for   comprehension,   c) value imitative speaking in  language learning for   effective communication. | The learner is guided to:  ∙ watch videos or pictures on  various sports and leisure   activities,   ∙ collaborate to present vocabulary  on names of various sports and  leisure activities,   ∙ match vocabulary to their   meaning,  ∙ team up to play a language game  on vocabulary or pictures   showing various sports and   leisure activities,   ∙ construct sentences orally using  vocabulary related to the theme, ∙ make oral presentations on how  to utilise leisure time,  ∙ imitate a fluent presenter of their  choice during their free time and  present in class or school   ceremonies/ event/ function. | How does   imitative   speaking help in  language   development? |

25

| Core competencies to be developed:  ∙ Communication and collaboration: This is enhanced as the learner builds on their listening skills when imitating a fluent  presenter of their choice and presents in class or school ceremonies/event/function.  ∙ Critical thinking and problem solving: This is enhanced as the learner embraces open mindedness when teaming up to play  a language game on vocabulary or pictures showing various sports and leisure activities. |
| :---- |
| **Values:**   ∙ Unity: This is enhanced as the learner displays a team spirit when playing a language game on vocabulary and pictures  showing various sports and leisure activities.  ∙ Peace: This is nurtured as the learner displays calmness when collaborating to present vocabulary on names of various  sports and leisure activities. |
| **Pertinent and Contemporary Issues (PCIs):**   Health promotion: This is enhanced as learners interact with texts on fitness and wellness from various sources. |

26

| Strand  | Sub Strand  | Specific Learning   Outcomes | Suggested Learning Experiences  | Suggested Key  Inquiry   Question(s) |
| ----- | ----- | ----- | ----- | :---- |
| **2.0 Reading**  | **2.4 Intensive   Reading: Short  Stories**  ∙ *Characters*  ∙ *Subject   matter/themes*  ∙ *Moral lessons* | By the end of the Sub Strand,  the learner should be able to: a) identify characters in a  story for information,  b) describe the themes in a  text for comprehension,  c) deduce the moral lessons  from a story,  d) enjoy reading Arabic  stories for lifelong   learning. | The learner is guided to:  ∙ team up to collect a variety of  short Arabic stories and share with  peers,  ∙ read Arabic stories from online or  offline sources,   ∙ work jointly to pick out characters  from a short Arabic story,  ∙ create a mind map to show the  various characters in the story,  ∙ make notes on the themes brought  out in the stories related to health  wellness and fitness,  ∙ discuss the moral lessons learnt  from the story read,   ∙ team up to form a reading club to  share information about stories  read. | How do stories  help in language  acquisition? |
| **Core competencies to be developed:**  ∙ Creativity and imagination: This is nurtured as the learner makes connections between concepts when creating a mind map  to show the various characters in the story.  ∙ Digital Literacy: This is promoted as the learner interacts with digital content when reading Arabic stories from online or  offline sources. |  |  |  |  |

27

| Values:  ∙ Love: This is embraced as the learner appreciates peers’ views when discussing the moral lessons learnt from the short  Arabic story read.  ∙ Patriotism: This is portrayed as the learner offers guidance to others when teaming up to collect a variety of short Arabic  stories and shares with peers. |
| :---- |
| **Pertinent and Contemporary Issues (PCIs):**   Preventive Health: The learner increases their knowledge on how to keep healthy as they read stories on health wellness and  fitness for promotion of a healthy lifestyle. |

28

| Strand  | Sub Strand  | Specific Learning Outcomes  | Suggested Learning Experiences  | Suggested Key   Inquiry   Question(s) |
| ----- | ----- | ----- | ----- | :---- |
| **3.0 Writing**  | **3.4 Social   Writing**  ∙ *Emails*   ∙ *Recipes*   ∙ *Advertisem ent*   ∙ *Posters* | By the end of the Sub Strand,  the learner should be able to:  a) discuss the features of  social writing for   information,  b) write social texts (*email,  recipe, advertisement,   poster*) creatively,  c) develop interest in social  writing for lifelong   learning. | The learner is guided to:  ∙ brainstorm on features and   examples of social writing texts,  ∙ team up to collect samples of  Arabic *emails, recipes,   advertisement and posters* from  online and offline sources,  ∙ discuss the format of the samples  collected,  ∙ write an email using correct format and language,   ∙ create a simple recipe on a healthy  meal to advocate for healthy living  (fitness and wellness),   ∙ design an advertisement related to  wellness and fitness and share,  ∙ design a poster providing   information advocating for fitness  and wellness and share with peers, ∙ team up to present their well prepared work in class,  ∙ display their advertisements and  posters in class. | How does social  writing promote  language   acquisition? |

29

| Core competencies to be developed:  ∙ Learning to learn: This is promoted as the learner carries out research to build on their learning experiences by collecting  samples of Arabic *emails, recipes, advertisement and posters* from online and offline sources.  ∙ Self \-efficacy: This is demonstrated as the learner executes assigned tasks when discussing the format of the samples  collected. |
| :---- |
| **Values:**  ∙ Social justice: This is developed as the learner accords each one an equal opportunity in the teams to present their well prepared work in class.  ∙ Responsibility: This is demonstrated as the learner exhibits self-drive in managing tasks when collecting samples of  Arabic *emails, recipes, advertisement and posters* from online and offline sources. |
| **Pertinent and Contemporary Issues (PCIs):**   Health promotion: This is promoted as learners interact with information and write texts on wellness and fitness.  |

30

| Strand  | Sub Strand  | Specific Learning Outcomes  | Suggested Learning Experiences  | Suggested Key  Inquiry   Question(s) |
| ----- | ----- | :---- | ----- | :---- |
| **4.0 Grammar**  | **4.4 Five   Special Nouns  *(Asmaa ul   Khamsa)*  أسماء الخمسة** | By the end of the sub strand,  the learner should be able to:  a) discuss the five special   nouns *(Asmaa ul Khamsa)* for information,  b) construct simple sentences  using the five special nouns  for effective   communication,  c) develop a positive attitude  towards learning the Arabic  language. | The learner is guided to:  ∙ team up to conduct research on the  five special nouns *(Asmaa ul   Khamsa)* from online or offline  sources,  ∙ list the five special nouns on  flashcards and share with peers, ∙ discuss the rules observed when  constructing a sentence using   *Asmaa ul Khamsa,*  ∙ construct sentences using *Asmaa  ul Khamsa*,   ∙ categorise the sentences into  groups from a wide variety   displayed to showing *Asmaa ul  Khamsa,*  ∙ write a paragraph using *Asmaa ul  Khamsa*. | How does   *Asmaa ul   Khamsa*   enhance   language   proficiency*?* |
| **Core competencies to be developed:**  ∙ Digital Literacy: This is promoted as the learner interacts with technology when conducting research on the five special  nouns *(Asmaa ul Khamsa)* from online or offline sources.  ∙ Critical thinking and problem solving: This is promoted as the learner follows simple instructions to solve problems when  categorising the sentences into groups showing *Asmaa ul Khamsa* from a wide variety*.* |  |  |  |  |

31

| Values:  ∙ Peace: This is developed as the learner displays tolerance with peers when discussing the rules observed when  constructing a sentence using *Asmaa ul Khamsa.*  ∙ Integrity: This is nurtured as the learner displays transparency when using digital technology to conduct research on the  meaning of *Asmaa ul Khamsa*. |
| :---- |
| **Pertinent and Contemporary Issues (PCIs):**   Social cohesion: This is promoted as the learner works jointly with peers by embracing each other’s strengths and  contributions to accomplish tasks and achieve goals. |

32

| ) َمدَر َس ) SCHOOL 5: THEMEِرس فِي ِكينيَا( KENYA IN SCHOOLS – ة )ال َم َدا |  |  |  |  |
| :---- | :---- | ----- | ----- | :---- |
| **Strand**  | **Sub Strand**  | **Specific Learning   Outcomes** | **Suggested Learning Experiences**  | **Suggested Key  Inquiry   Question(s)** |
| **1.0 Listening  and Speaking**  | **1.5 Conversational  skills**  ∙ *Etiquette*  ∙ *Turn taking*  ∙ *Disagreeing   politely* | By the end of the Sub  Strand, the learner should  be able to:   a) describe practices  showing etiquette in   conversations for   information,  b) apply turn taking and  disagree politely in   conversations for   effective   communication,  c) acknowledge the role  of etiquette as a   conversational skill   for effective   communication  | The learner is guided to:  ∙ watch a video recording on a  conversation about the education  system in Kenya,  ∙ with peers, brainstorm on common  mistakes made during  conversations,  ∙ make a list of words used to show  etiquette in conversations and share  with peers,  ∙ conduct research on ways of  ensuring etiquette in conversations, ∙ team up to write words and phrases  used to disagree politely on   flashcards,  ∙ conduct an interview jointly while  disagreeing politely and turn taking, ∙ participate in a dialogue on the  education systems in Kenya with a  resource person while showing   etiquette and turn taking. | Why should   one observe   etiquette in a   conversation? |

33

| Core competencies to be developed:  ∙ Citizenship: This is promoted as the learner engages effectively with others when brainstorming on common mistakes in  conversations.  ∙ Learning to learn: This is enhanced as the learner learns independently when conducting research on ways of ensuring  etiquette in conversations. |
| :---- |
| **Values:**   ∙ Respect: This is promoted as the learner shows positive regard for self and others when conducting an interview jointly  while observing conversation etiquette.  ∙ Social justice: This is promoted as the learner accords equal opportunities to others when participating in a dialogue on the  education systems in Kenya with a resource person. |
| **Pertinent and Contemporary Issues (PCIs):**   Citizenship education: This is enhanced as the learner embraces social cohesion when interacting with video recordings and  resource persons on the education system in Kenya and participates in a dialogue about it. |

34

| Strand  | Sub Strand  | Specific Learning   Outcomes | Suggested Learning Experiences  | Suggested Key  Inquiry   Question(s) |
| ----- | ----- | ----- | ----- | :---- |
| **2.0 Reading**  | **2.5 Reading for  Comprehension**  ∙ *Direct and   Inferential   Questions*  ∙ *Making   Connections* | By the end of the Sub Strand,  the learner should be able to:  a) respond to questions from   a text for comprehension,  b) make connections   between events and   people in a text to real   life,   c) acknowledge the role of  Arabic texts for lifelong  learning. | The learner is guided to:  ∙ read a range of texts about the  education system in Kenya,  ∙ answer direct questions from texts, ∙ work jointly to answer inferential  (indirect) questions from texts,   ∙ discuss how the events in a story  relate to real life experiences,   ∙ brainstorm on how the people in the  story relate to real life,  ∙ prepare a visual organiser to show  connections between events and  people to real life,   ∙ role play a section of the texts read  with peers in class,   ∙ read selected texts on the education  system in Kenya and compare and  contrast it with other countries,   ∙ make notes on the main ideas to  summarise the texts related to the  theme. | Why should we  make   connections   between events  in texts and real  life? |

35

| Core competencies to be developed:  ∙ Communication and collaboration: This is enhanced as the learner speaks engagingly using facts and examples when  brainstorming on how the people in the story relate to real life.  ∙ Citizenship: This is promoted as the learner accumulates information when reading a range of texts about the education  system in Kenya and compares and contrasts it with other countries. |
| :---- |
| **Values**:  ∙ Peace: This is nurtured as the learner displays tolerance towards peers when working jointly to answer inferential  (indirect) questions from the text.   ∙ Responsibility: This is demonstrated as the learner respects others’ views when comparing and contrasting education  systems in Kenya to that of other countries. |
| **Pertinent and Contemporary Issues (PCIs):**   ∙ Citizenship education: This is addressed as the learners interact with texts in an effort to understand other education  systems in relation to that of their own country. |

36

| Strand  | Sub Strand  | Specific Learning Outcomes  | Suggested Learning Experiences  | Suggested Key  Inquiry   Question(s) |
| ----- | ----- | ----- | ----- | :---- |
| **3.0 Writing**  | **3.5: Creative  Writing:   Compositions**  ∙ *Format*  ∙ *Language*  ∙ *Stylistic   devices   (proverbs   and sayings)* | By the end of the Sub Strand,  the learner should be able to: a) discuss the format and   language in creative texts  for information,  b) write a composition using  creative language for   effective communication, c) develop interest in   creative writing to express  ideas. | The learner is guided to:  ∙ collect various sample compositions from online or offline sources,   ∙ brainstorm on the format of an  imaginative composition  (*introduction, body, conclusion*),  ∙ team up to create a collection of  Arabic proverbs and sayings,  ∙ work jointly to list Arabic proverbs  and sayings with their meaning,  ∙ write an imaginative composition  based on a picture story related to the  theme,  ∙ write an imaginative composition (*not  less than 400 words*) using accurate  and creative language,   ∙ jointly organise a class creative  writing contest and write   compositions while using the   proverbs and sayings learnt. | How can you  enhance  creativity in   composition   writing? |
| **Core competencies to be developed:**  ∙ Digital Literacy: This is promoted as the learner interacts with digital content when collecting various sample  compositions from online or offline sources. |  |  |  |  |

37

| ∙ Creativity and Imagination: This is developed as the learner connects different ideas when writing an imaginative  composition using accurate and creative language. |
| :---- |
| **Values:**  ∙ Unity: The spirit of cooperation is enhanced as the learner collaborates with peers to list Arabic proverbs and sayings with  their meaning.  ∙ Integrity: This is demonstrated as the learner displays commitment to duty when writing compositions while using the proverbs and sayings learnt and displaying them in class. |
| **Pertinent and Contemporary Issues (PCIs):**   Civic Responsibility: This is promoted as the learners interact with texts and knowledge relating to the education system in  Kenya. |

38

| Strand  | Sub Strand  | Specific Learning   Outcomes | Suggested Learning Experiences  | Suggested Key  Inquiry   Question(s) |
| ----- | ----- | ----- | :---- | :---- |
| **4.0 Grammar**  | **4.5 Five   Special   Verbs *(Af   ’aal al   Khamsa)*  َ  فعَال ال َخم َسة  أ** | By the end of the sub   strand, the learner should be  able to:   a) discuss the five special  verbs *(Af ’aal al   Khamsa)* for   information,   b) construct simple   sentences using the five  special verbs for   effective   communication,   c) develop a positive   attitude towards   learning the Arabic   language. | The learner is guided to:  ∙ research on the five special verbs in  Arabic grammar,   ∙ list the five special verbs *(Af ’aal al  Khamsa)* on flashcards,  ∙ use a substitution table to make  sentences with *Af ’aal al Khamsa,* ∙ construct sentences using *Af ’aal al  Khamsa*,   ∙ read a passage on Education systems  in Kenya,  ∙ underline the *Af ’aal al Khamsa* from  a given passage,  ∙ write a paragraph using *Af ’aal al  Khamsa,*  ∙ peer assess each other’s paragraphs  for correctness,  ∙ organise their well written   paragraphs in the portfolio. | Why is *Af ’ aal al  Khamsa* unique  from other verbs  in Arabic*?* |
| **Core competencies to be developed:**  ∙ Self-efficacy: This is developed as the learner shows concerted attention when reading a passage on Education systems in  Kenya. |  |  |  |  |

39

| ∙ Communication and collaboration: This is nurtured as the learner enhances their writing skills when ordering their ideas in a paragraph using *Af ’ aal al Khamsa.* |
| :---- |
| **Values:**  ∙ Responsibility: This is promoted as the learner exhibits self-drive in managing tasks when organising their well written  paragraphs in the portfolio.  ∙ Respect: This is enhanced as the learner shows positive regard for others when peer assessing each other’s paragraphs for  correctness. |
| **Pertinent and Contemporary Issues (PCIs):**   Global Citizenship: This is promoted as the learners interact with the Arabic language and its grammatical structures to  enhance their proficiency and build their communication. |

40

| ) فِي البَنك( BANK THE AT – م الَع َمال( )عَ الَ BUSINESS OF WORLD THE : 6 THEME |  |  |  |  |
| ----- | ----- | ----- | ----- | :---- |
| **Strand**  | **Sub Strand**  | **Specific Learning Outcomes**  | **Suggested Learning Experiences**  | **Suggested Key   Inquiry   Question(s)** |
| **1.0 Listening  and Speaking**  | **1.6 Selective  Listening**   ∙ *Main idea*  ∙ *Supporting  ideas*  | By the end of the Sub Strand,  the learner should be able to: a) identify the main idea  from a given oral text for  information,  b) extract the supporting  ideas from an oral text   for information,  c) appreciate selective   listening skills when   looking for information. | The learner is guided to:  ∙ listen to an audio recording on  banking services and world   currencies,   ∙ identify the banking activities  and world currencies cited   from the audio recording,   ∙ work jointly to pick out the  various vocabulary related to  banking services and world   currencies,  ∙ pick out the main idea from an  oral text and share with peers, ∙ team up to identify the   supporting ideas from an oral  text,   ∙ prepare a mind map to present  the main idea and its   supporting details, | How does selective  listening promote  communication  skills? |

41

|  |  |  | ∙ organise the mind maps in a  portfolio,  ∙ role play a banking scene with  peers displaying various   banking activities and services. |  |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | ----- | :---- |
| **Core competencies to be developed:**  ∙ Self-efficacy: This is enhanced as the learner clearly executes procedural tasks when preparing a mind map to present the  main idea and its supporting details.  ∙ Creativity and imagination: This is nurtured as the learner gains new perspectives on undertaking tasks as they role play a  banking scene with peers. |  |  |  |  |
| **Values**:   ∙ Responsibility: This is embraced as the learner engages in assigned roles and duties when role playing a banking scene  with peers.  ∙ Unity: This is enhanced as the learner respects others’ opinions when working jointly to pick out the various vocabulary  related to banking services and world currencies. |  |  |  |  |
| **Pertinent and Contemporary Issues (PCIs):**   Economic issues (financial literacy): This is promoted as learners use online or offline resources and discuss different banking  services and world currencies. |  |  |  |  |

42

| Strand  | Sub Strand  | Specific Learning   Outcomes | Suggested Learning Experiences  | Suggested Key  Inquiry   Question(s) |
| ----- | ----- | ----- | ----- | :---- |
| **2.0 Reading**  | **2.6 Independent  Reading**   ∙ *Tracing reading  materials*  ∙ *Summarising \- Main ideas*  ∙ *Tracking   progress* | By the end of the Sub   Strand the learner should  be able to:  a) trace Arabic texts for  independent reading,  b) summarise information  from a text for   effective   communication,  c) track reading progress  for lifelong learning,  d) enjoy reading Arabic  texts for lifelong   learning. | The learner is guided to:   ∙ work jointly to collect varied  Arabic texts and stories for   reading,   ∙ read texts and stories based on  banking services and world   currencies,  ∙ make summary notes on the main  ideas from the texts and stories  read,   ∙ prepare a reading log to track  reading progress,   ∙ peer assess each other’s reading  logs to share ideas on texts read,  ∙ collaborate to form reading clubs  to share information read from  texts and stories (*storybooks,   newspapers*),  ∙ team up to organise an Arabic class  library session. | How does   independent   reading develop  language skills? |
| **Core competencies to be developed:**  ∙ Critical thinking and problem solving: This is promoted as the learner embraces evaluation and decision making when  collaborating to form reading clubs to share information read from texts and stories. |  |  |  |  |

43

| ∙ Learning to learn: This is promoted as the learner embraces open-mindedness in receiving constructive feedback as they  prepare and keep a reading log to track reading progress. |
| :---- |
| **Values**:   ∙ Integrity: This is demonstrated as the learner embraces honesty by avoiding conflict of interest when peer assessing each  other’s reading logs to share ideas on texts read.  ∙ Social Justice: This is nurtured as the learner fosters inclusivity when teaming up to organise an Arabic class library  session. |
| **Pertinent and Contemporary Issues (PCIs):**   Self-management: This is addressed as the learners interact with information and texts on banking services and how to manage  finances. |

44

| Strand  | Sub Strand  | Specific Learning   Outcomes | Suggested Learning Experiences  | Suggested Key  Inquiry   Question(s) |
| ----- | :---- | ----- | ----- | :---- |
| **3.0 Writing**  | **3.6 Functional   Writing: Formal  Letters**  ∙ *Format*  ∙ *Formal   Language* | By the end of the Sub   Strand, the learner should  be able to:   a) identify features of a  formal letter for   information,  b) apply correct format to  write a formal letter for  effective   communication,  c) value the role of formal  letters for lifelong   learning. | The learner is guided to:  ∙ team up to collect samples of  formal letters from online or offline  sources,  ∙ work jointly to discuss the format of an Arabic formal letter from   samples (*job application letter,  offer acceptance letter,   recommendation letter, scholarship  application letter, etc.*),  ∙ make notes on the format of a  formal letter,  ∙ write an application letter seeking  for a job in a bank within their   locality,  ∙ peer assess each other’s work for  correctness,  ∙ organise their well written formal  letters in a portfolio,  ∙ practice writing formal letters for  different purposes (*job application,  offer acceptance, recommendation,  scholarship application, etc.*).  | Why are formal  letters   considered   important in the  world today? |

45

| Core competencies to be developed:  ∙ Citizenship: This is promoted as the learner exploits readiness for opportunities on professional activities when writing an  application letter seeking for a job in a bank within their locality.  ∙ Communication and collaboration: This is developed as the learner speaks engagingly when discussing the format of an  Arabic formal letter from samples collected. |
| :---- |
| **Values**:  ∙ Respect: This is nurtured as the learner embraces open mindedness in receiving constructive feedback during peer  assessment.  ∙ Peace: This is demonstrated as the learner embraces respect for diversity when working with peers to organise their well  written formal letters in a portfolio. |
| **Pertinent and Contemporary Issues (PCIs):**   Social Cohesion: This is addressed as the learners work jointly with peers to accomplish group tasks. |

46

| Strand  | Sub Strand  | Specific Learning   Outcomes | Suggested Learning Experiences  | Suggested Key  Inquiry   Question(s) |
| ----- | :---- | ----- | ----- | :---- |
| **4.0 Grammar**  | **4.6 Numbers  and Quantity *(Al- ’adad wal  Ma’duud)*  العَ َدد َوال َمعُدود** | By the end of the sub  strand, the learner should  be able to:   a) describe the rules  associated with   numbers and quantity  *(Al- ’adad wal   Ma’duud)* for   information,  b) apply vocabulary on  numbers and quantity  in sentence   construction,  c) appreciate the role of  the Arabic numerals   in language   acquisition. | The learner is guided to:  ∙ team up to recite numbers in Arabic, ∙ watch a video recording or listen to a  resource person on the rules   associated with expressing quantity in Arabic i.e. rules on:  \- 1-2  \- 3-9  \- 11-19  \- 10,20,30,40,50,60,70,80,90  ∙ identify objects in the class and  express them in their quantities,  ∙ team up to prepare flashcards  showing *Al- ’adad wal Ma’duud* of  various objects in their surroundings,  ∙ work jointly to create a table  showing objects and their quantities, ∙ collaborate to solve crossword  puzzles on *Al- ’adad wal Ma’duud*, ∙ write a paragraph with vocabulary  related to *Al- ’adad wal Ma’duud.* ∙ peer assess each other’s paragraphs  for correctness. | How does   identification of  numbers and   quantities in the  Arabic language  differ from other  languages? |

47

| Core competencies to be developed:  ∙ Digital Literacy: This is promoted as the learner interacts with technology when watching a video recording or listening to  a resource person on the rules associated with expressing quantity in Arabic.  ∙ Citizenship: This is enhanced as the learner appreciates and encourages teamwork and support when collaborating to solve  crossword puzzles on *Al- ’adad wal Ma’duud.* |
| :---- |
| **Values:**  ∙ Integrity: This is enhanced as the learner exhibits fairness when sharing resources to prepare flashcards showing *Al- ’adad  wal Ma’duud* of various objects in their surroundings.  ∙ Social Justice: This is promoted as the learner accords equal opportunities to others when working jointly to create a table  showing objects and their quantity. |
| **Pertinent and Contemporary Issues (PCIs):**   Good governance: This is promoted as the learner takes up leadership responsibilities or follows given guidance when  working in teams to accomplish tasks. |

48  
**SUGGESTED ASSESSMENT RUBRIC** 

| STRAND : LISTENING AND SPEAKING |  |  |  |  |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
|  **Level Indicator** | **Exceeding  Expectations** | **Meeting   Expectations** | **Approaching  Expectations** | **Below Expectations** |
| Ability to use   vocabulary and   expressions to   probe and engage in oral interactions | The learner uses all   targeted vocabulary and  expressions to probe and  engage in oral   interactions; uses a variety of vocabulary and   expressions synonymous  to the targeted ones | The learner uses targeted vocabulary  and expressions to  probe and engage in oral interactions | The learner uses most of  the targeted vocabulary and expressions to probe and  engage in oral interactions | The learner uses few of the  targeted vocabulary and  expressions to probe and  engage in oral interactions |
| Ability to maintain  correct spoken   language patterns (pronunciation,   intonation, stress  and pace) in oral  expression | The learner maintains  correct pronunciation,  intonation, stress and pace at all times in oral   expression. Makes   successful attempts at  using idiomatic   expressions | The learner   maintains correct  pronunciation,   intonation, stress  and pace at all times in oral expression.  Communication is  clear and seamless | The learner maintains  correct pronunciation,  intonation, stress and pace most of the time in oral  expression. Makes a few  errors at times that may  interfere with   communication | The learner maintains  correct pronunciation,  intonation, stress and pace on rare occasions in oral  expression. Makes many  errors most of the time that  interfere with   communication |
| Ability to respond  to questions and  prompts to show  comprehension and  | The learner responds to all questions and prompts to  show comprehension and  engagement and goes  | The learner   responds to all   questions and   prompts to show  | The learner responds to  most of the questions and  prompts to show   comprehension and   engagement | The learner responds to  very few questions and  prompts to show   comprehension and   engagement |

49 

| STRAND : LISTENING AND SPEAKING |  |  |  |  |
| :---- | :---- | ----- | :---- | :---- |
|  **Level Indicator** | **Exceeding  Expectations** | **Meeting   Expectations** | **Approaching  Expectations** | **Below Expectations** |
| engagement in oral  interactions | further to give appropriate  illustrations and examples. | comprehension and  engagement |  |  |
| Ability to employ  gestures and facial  expressions to   enhance oral   interactions | The learner employs  varied and appropriate  gestures and facial   expressions in oral   expressions whenever  required. Is sensitive to  cultural diversity and  adapts non-verbal cues to  diverse cultural contexts | The learner   employs adequate  gestures and facial  expressions to   complement oral  expressions   whenever required | The learner employs many gestures and facial   expressions to complement  oral expressions when  required. A few gestures  and facial expressions used  do not rhyme with the  intended communication | The learner employs a few  gestures and facial   expressions to complement  oral expressions when  required. Many of the  gestures and facial   expressions used do not  rhyme with the intended  communication  |
| Ability to display  active listening   when participating in oral interactions | The learner always   displays active listening  when participating in  meaningful oral   interactions with people in their surroundings.   Encourages others to  engage in oral interactions | The learner always  displays active   listening when   participating in   meaningful oral  interactions with  people in their   surroundings | The learner is at times  hesitant to display active  listening when participating in meaningful oral   interactions with people in  their surroundings | The learner is mostly   hesitant to display active  listening when participating in meaningful oral   interactions with people in  their surroundings |

50 

| STRAND: READING |  |  |  |  |
| ----- | :---- | :---- | ----- | :---- |
|  **Level Indicator** | **Exceeding Expectations**  | **Meeting   Expectations** | **Approaching ExpectationsBelow Expectations** |  |
| Ability to   pronounce words in  contexts for   accuracy in reading | The learner maintains  accurate pronunciation of  words in simple texts on  familiar topics to convey  the intended meanings.  Makes no errors of   pronunciation even for  text on unfamiliar topics | The learner   maintains accurate  pronunciation of  words in simple  texts on familiar  topics to convey the intended meaning | The learner maintains  accurate pronunciation of  words in simple texts on  familiar topics. Few errors are made which may   hamper intended meaning | The learner makes attempts to maintain accurate   pronunciation of words in  simple texts on familiar  topics. Many errors are  made which may hamper  intended meaning |
| Ability to read with  fluency   (smoothness, pace,  pauses and   intonation) | The learner reads   simple texts with   natural and smooth   flow and at an   appropriate pace all   through the text.   Pays attention to all   punctuations and   applies appropriate   pauses and   intonation to express  intention and   emotions. Makes no  | The learner reads  simple texts with  natural and smooth  flow and at an   appropriate pace all  through the text.  Pays attention to all  punctuations and  applies appropriate  pauses and   intonation to   express intention  and emotions.  | The learner reads simple  text with natural and   smooth flow and at an  appropriate pace through  most parts of the text. Pays  attention to all punctuations and applies appropriate  pauses and intonation to  express intention and  emotions. Makes errors in  texts on unfamiliar topics  | The learner makes attempts to read a simple text. Pays  attention to some   punctuations and applies  appropriate pauses and  intonation to express   intention and emotions.  Makes errors in texts on  unfamiliar topics  |

 

51 

| STRAND: READING |  |  |  |  |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | ----- | :---- |
|  **Level Indicator** | **Exceeding Expectations**  | **Meeting   Expectations** | **Approaching ExpectationsBelow Expectations** |  |
|  | errors at all even in   texts on unfamiliar   topics | Makes minimal  errors in texts on  unfamiliar topics  and auto-corrects  self in the few   errors made |  |  |

| STRAND: WRITING |  |  |  |  |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
|  **Level Indicator** |  |  | **Exceeding Expectations Meeting Expectations Approaching Expectations Below Expectations** |  |
| Ability to write  clear and readable  texts (*Neatness and legibility*) | The learner writes   readable texts paying  attention to neatness all  through. Letters in all  the words are correctly  sized and shaped within  the lines. Words are  correctly spaced within  sentences all through.  | The learner writes  readable texts paying  attention to neatness  all through. Letters in  all the words are   correctly sized and  shaped within the   lines. Words are   correctly spaced | The learner writes readable  texts paying attention to  neatness in most parts of  the text. Letters in most of  the words are correctly  sized and shaped within the lines. Some inconsistent  spacing of words within  sentences is visible | The learner writes texts that are not easily readable.  Neatness is not maintained  all through. Letters in most  of the words are incorrectly sized and shaped within the lines. Frequent inconsistent spacing of words within  sentences is visible |

 

 

 

52 

| STRAND: WRITING |  |  |  |  |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
|  **Level Indicator** |  |  | **Exceeding Expectations Meeting Expectations Approaching Expectations Below Expectations** |  |
|  | General presentation of  the text is excellent. | within sentences all  through |  |  |
| Ability to write  texts using correct  language structures and vocabulary | The learner writes   simple texts while   maintaining spelling and grammatical accuracy all through. Sentence   structures are correct all  the time while using a  variety of rich   vocabulary appropriate  to the context | The learner writes  simple texts while  maintaining spelling  and grammatical     accuracy all through.  Sentence structures are correct all the time  while using adequate  vocabulary appropriate to the context | The learner writes simple  texts while maintaining  spelling and grammatical  accuracy in a large portion  of the texts. Sentence  structures are correct most  of the time. Uses limited  vocabulary appropriate to  the context   | The learner writes simple  texts but is unable to   maintain spelling and  grammatical accuracy all  through. Sentence   structures are incorrect  most of the time. Uses very limited vocabulary   appropriate to the context |

53 

| STRAND: GRAMMAR |  |  |  |  |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | ----- | :---- |
|  **Level Indicator** | **Exceeding  Expectations** | **Meeting Expectations**  | **Approaching Expectations Below Expectations** |  |
| Ability to apply  mechanics of   Writing in texts | Learner applies varied  and correct mechanics  of writing in texts all  the time. Meaning is  clearly communicated | Learner applies correct  mechanics of writing in texts all the time.  Meaning is clearly  communicated | Learner applies correct  mechanics of writing in  texts most of the time.  Some errors impede   reading and meaning | Learner applies incorrect  mechanics of writing in  texts. The reader can only  guess meaning |
| Ability to use   grammatical   structures in texts | Excellent use of   grammatical structures in a variety of texts.  All of the grammatical  rules are meticulously  followed | Good use of   grammatical structures  in a text. All of the  grammatical rules are  followed | There are moderate errors  of grammar in a text. Most  of the grammatical rules  are followed. | There are numerous errors  of grammar in a text. Few  of the grammatical rules  are followed. |

54

| **2.0 Reading.**  | ∙ Reading texts.  ∙ Flashcards.  ∙ Pictures.  ∙ Images.  | ∙ Reading aloud.  ∙ Discussions.  ∙ Observations.  ∙ Quizzes. | ∙ School Open Days.  ∙ Kenya Music Festival. ∙ Arabic language Clubs. ∙ Exchange Programs. |
